frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: decision variable compared to a criterion to categorize perceptual reports

frameworks.name: fixed-criterion model of subjective inflation
frameworks.one_line: criterion for subjective visibility does not adjust to sensory uncertainty

frameworks.name: flexible-criterion decision-making
frameworks.one_line: criterion adapts to sensory uncertainty including under inattention

frameworks.name: metacognition of perceptual awareness
frameworks.one_line: criterion-crossing taken to mark conscious access and subjective visibility

core_question: Do existing empirical findings on subjective inflation actually require a fixed perceptual decision criterion?
key_assumptions: perceptual decisions arise from comparing a decision variable to a criterion
subjective visibility reports reflect a criterion on a graded internal signal
criterion behavior under uncertainty is testable across consciousness and decision paradigms
manipulations: formal comparison of fixed vs. flexible criterion accounts
varying mapping between decision variable and stimulus feature space
varying task incentives to adjust criterion with response variance
key_findings: previous subjective-inflation evidence is consistent with both fixed and flexible criteria
without a clear decision-to-feature mapping, criterion flexibility is unidentifiable
without an incentive to adjust, observers may keep the criterion fixed even if flexible
interpretations: the fixed-criterion model of subjective inflation requires re-thinking
specific task design conditions are needed to infer criterion flexibility
limitations_noted: analysis is mathematical/conceptual rather than a new empirical test
